attributes.stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents because he has been limping for the past two weeks and is complaining of pain in his left hip. There is no history of trauma or fever and he is otherwise in good health. On examination, his blood pressure is 112/74 mmHg, pulse 86/min, and temperature 37.2oC. His height is in the 75th percentile and his weight is in the 90th percentile. There is hip pain on palpation and a limitation of hip range of motion, especially internal rotation and flexion. His blood investigations are within normal limits. An AP radiograph of his bilateral hips is shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Hip pain in a 14-year-old boy whose weight is in the 90th percentile with an X-ray finding such as the one shown in this case suggests a diagnosis of slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE). In the normal AP view, a line drawn along the superior femoral neck (Klein's line) intersects the lateral portion of the femoral head (Image). In a patient with SCFE, the line passes outside of the epiphysis or just at its superior edge (Trethowan sign).

In SCFE, the femoral epiphysis slips posteriorly, resulting in a limp and impaired internal rotation. The typical patient is an obese child in early adolescence. The mean age of presentation is 12 years in females and 13.5 years in males. Patients may present with acute hip pain and an inability to walk, often after minor trauma. Plain radiographs will reveal apparent posterior displacement of the femoral epiphysis. Southwick Slip Angle is used to identify and classify SCFE.

Occult fractures (child abuse): Metaphyseal corners and/or multiple fractures with different stages of healing are highly suggestive of abuse. The age of this patient, the absence of warning signs of abuse (burn marks, contusions, lacerations, not making eye contact, etc.) and lack of radiological findings make occult fracture (child abuse) an unlikely diagnosis. Children younger than five years of age are at the highest risk of child abuse.

Legg-Calve-Perthes disease has an acute or insidious onset in children between the ages of 3 and 12 years. Initial radiographs are often normal, but as the disease progresses, extensive destruction of both proximal femoral epiphyses occurs, with collapse, flattening, widening, and fragmentation.

A child with septic arthritis of the hip will present with fever and an overall ill-looking appearance. Physical examination will show significant reduction in range of movement and pain. Blood work will show leukocytosis and an elevated C-RP. Radiographs will show soft tissue swelling, widening of the joint space, increased opacity within the joint, subluxation, or erosion of subchondral bone.

Transient synovitis, also called toxic synovitis, commonly presents acutely (within 1-3 days of onset) in children aged 3–8 years. Children appear well on general examination. Low grade fever may be present. Plain film radiography may be normal or may indicate a joint effusion.
learningPoints: In slipped capital femoral epiphysis, the femoral epiphysis slips posteriorly.
The mean age of presentation is 12 years in girls and 13.5 years in boys.
In a patient with SCFE, the line drawn along the superior femoral neck (Klein's line) passes outside of the epiphysis or just at its superior edge (Trethowan sign).
references: Kathryn Johns; Ahmed Mabrouk; Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis [Internet]. StatPearls Publishing LLC. Last Update: November 14, 2022; cited 2023 March 8. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538302/
Bennett OM, Namnyak SS. Acute septic arthritis of the hip joint in infancy and childhood. Clin Orthop Relat Res 1992; :123.
Caird MS, Flynn JM, Leung YL, et al. Factors distinguishing septic arthritis from transient synovitis of the hip in children. A prospective study. J Bone Joint Surg Am 2006; 88:1251.
Haueisen DC, Weiner DS, Weiner SD. The characterization of "transient synovitis of the hip" in children. J Pediatr Orthop 1986; 6:11.
Neal DC, Alford TH, Moualeu A, et al. Prevalence of Obesity in Patients With Legg-Calvé-Perthes Disease. J Am Acad Orthop Surg 2016; 24:660.
Herngren B, Stenmarker M, Vavruch L, Hagglund G. Slipped capital femoral epiphysis: a population-based study. BMC Musculoskelet Disord 2017; 18:304.

Manz N, Krieg AH, Heininger U, Ritz N. Evaluation of the current use of imaging modalities and pathogen detection in children with acute osteomyelitis and septic arthritis. Eur J Pediatr 2018; 177:1071.
Perry DC, Metcalfe D, Lane S, Turner S. Childhood Obesity and Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis. Pediatrics 2018; 142.
Bloomberg TJ, Nuttall J, Stoker DJ. Radiology in early slipped femoral capital epiphysis. Clin Radiol 1978; 29:657.
